content:
High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation Boyuan Hao a, Zhiwei Jiang b

, Andrew Livingston b, Kang Li a,* a Barrer Centre, Department of Chemical Engineering, Imperial College London, Exhibition Road, London, SW7 2AZ, United Kingdomb School of Engineering and Materials Science, Queen Mary University of London, Joseph Priestley Building, Mile End Road, London, E1 4NS, United Kingdom

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:COF membranesOrganic solvent nanofiltration (OSN)Interfacial polymerization (IP)Pharmaceutical separation

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: A B S T R A C T Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) provides an energy-efficient approach for pharmaceutical fractionation, but its broader implementation is restricted by the inherent permeability–selectivity trade-off. Here, we report a COF membrane fabricated through an in-situ interfacial polymerization (IP) strategy. Unlike conventional IP, this insitu method enables direct COF growth on a porous support or via a COF interlayer, eliminating transfer steps and ensuring robust adhesion. By tuning the polymerization temperature, the membrane thickness can be pre­cisely regulated, resulting in high rejection of vitamin B12, rifampicin, and eriochrome black T (95.4–99.0%) while maintaining permeation of their respective precursors of 5,6-dimethylbenzimidazole, 1-amino-4-methyl­piperazine, and 4-nitrophenol (8.8–15.3%). The interlayer architecture preserves the rejection performance while doubling the solvent permeance (e.g., methanol: 21.7 →42.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) and increasing the phar­maceutical mixture processing rates. Overall, this work establishes a sequential in-situ IP route for versatile COF membrane fabrication and demonstrates that a COF interlayer design can effectively mitigate the long-standing OSN permeability–selectivity trade-off, enabling high-flux and high-efficiency pharmaceutical separations.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: 1. Introduction Owing to their high efficiency, energy savings, and ease of operation, membrane-based processes have found broad application across numerous separation fields, including gas separation, water purifica­tion, and organic solvent separation. Organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN), a nanoscale membrane technology used to separate solutes from organic solvents, has shown particularly strong potential in pharma­ceutical and chemical manufacturing [1]. In pharmaceutical manufacturing, efficient separation and purifica­tion are essential, as active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) are commonly synthesized and processed in organic media containing re­sidual precursors, intermediates, and process-related by-products [2]. Inadequate removal of these impurities may adversely affect drug quality, efficacy and safety, thereby posing potential health risks [3]. OSNs have emerged as promising alternatives for API separation and purification, enabling precise molecular discrimination based on size differences between APIs and undesired impurities (e.g., residual pre­cursors or by-products) [4]. Compared with conventional separation technologies such as distillation, crystallization, and chromatography, OSNs operate under mild conditions, reducing energy consumption, preserving API bioactivity, and allowing solvent recycling rather than relying on large volumes of fresh solvent [5]. Despite these advantages, the broader adoption of OSNs remains limited by two key challenges.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ther than relying on large volumes of fresh solvent [5]. Despite these advantages, the broader adoption of OSNs remains limited by two key challenges. First, amorphous polymer membranes are inherently restricted by a permeability–selectivity trade-off [6]. Second, chemical instability and swelling in aggressive organic solvents (e.g., polar aprotic solvents or aromatic hydrocarbons) can distort pore structures and degrade performance [6]. Addressing these issues re­quires new membrane materials with ordered nanoscale channels and robust solvent resistance. Covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have emerged as particularly promising candidates for OSN membranes because of their crystalline structures, well-defined nanochannels, and excellent chemical stability in harsh solvents, offering a structural advantage over amorphous polymeric membranes [7]. Their periodic architectures provide uniform pore sizes for precise molecular sieving, whereas their high porosity and vertically aligned channels enable rapid solvent transport [8]. More­over, robust linkages (e.g., β-ketoenamine) of 2D COFs combined with interlayer π–π stacking and hydrogen-bonding networks impart strong resistance to swelling, preserving pore dimensions even in polar aprotic, aromatic, and chlorinated solvents [9]. These attributes make COFs

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: Available online 13 February 2026 0376-7388/ 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). ideal for achieving both high permeance and selectivity in pharmaceu­tical separations, helping overcome the permeability–selectivity trade-off that traditionally limits OSN performance. Although COF membranes have received increasing attention in recent years [10–14], fabricating highly crystalline COF membranes with ordered one-dimensional nanochannels that deliver high separation perfor­mance remains challenging. Interfacial polymerization (IP) has emerged as a highly effective and reliable method for fabricating COF membranes [15–18]. Compared with direct polymerization (DP), IP provides enhanced controllability because the reaction is restricted to the interface of two immiscible phases, each containing one of the monomers. [19]. As a result, IP produces relatively thin COF layers in the order of hundreds of nano­meters [19], in contrast to the tens to hundreds of micrometers typically generated by DP [20,21]. To date, a common approach for COF membrane fabrication via IP involves first synthesizing a self-standing film at the interface between two immiscible phases, followed by its transfer onto a porous support.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ia IP involves first synthesizing a self-standing film at the interface between two immiscible phases, followed by its transfer onto a porous support. While this method eliminates the impact of support, the resulting COF layers are typically only tens to hundreds of nanometers thin [18,22], and the transfer process may introduce defects or cracks. To overcome these limitations, an in-situ IP can be employed to produce a thinner COF layer directly on a support, avoiding damage during transfer. Several recent studies have demonstrated that this strategy enables the fabrication of high-performance COF membranes under mild, room-temperature conditions, such as TpPa [23] and TpHz [24], etc. However, despite these advances, the influence of key fabrication pa­rameters on COF membrane formation remains insufficiently under­stood. Moreover, rational strategies for further enhancing membrane performance, particularly in terms of balancing permeance and selec­tivity, are still underexplored and warrant systematic investigation. Herein, we report two strategies for developing COF membranes via in-situ IP. In the first strategy, two immiscible phases containing the monomers 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp) and 2,5-diaminobenzene­sulfonic acid (Pa-SO3H), together with their respective activators, are placed on opposite sides of a substrate. This configuration enables direct formation of the TpPa-SO3H COF membrane on the substrate surface with strong adhesion.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: pposite sides of a substrate. This configuration enables direct formation of the TpPa-SO3H COF membrane on the substrate surface with strong adhesion. And the effect of polymerization temperature on the formation of COF membrane was systematically studied. In the second strategy, which is designed to enhance membrane permeation performance, a TpBd-(SO3H)2 COF interlayer is first formed on the substrate by introducing Tp and 2,2′-benzidinedisulfonic acid (Bd- (SO3H)2) on opposite sides of the substrate, followed by a second pair of immiscible phases containing Tp and Pa-SO3H to build a TpPa-SO3H layer atop the interlayer, yielding a hierarchical TpPa-SO3H/TpBd- (SO3H)2/nylon membrane architecture. The resulting membranes were systematically characterized in terms of their crystal structure, pore size, and surface properties. Their permeation and separation performance were evaluated using dyes and three pharmaceutical–precursor pairs were selected as model systems to assess their separation efficiency. Overall, this work establishes a versatile sequential in-situ IP approach for COF membrane fabrication and demonstrates that incor­porating a COF interlayer can effectively mitigate the long-standing permeability–selectivity trade-off in OSNs, enabling high-flux, high-ef­ficiency separations relevant to pharmaceutical manufacturing. 2. Methodology 2.1. Materials Nylon 66 membranes with a nominal pore size of 0.1 μm were pur­chased from Sterlitech (UK).

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: harmaceutical manufacturing. 2. Methodology 2.1. Materials Nylon 66 membranes with a nominal pore size of 0.1 μm were pur­chased from Sterlitech (UK). Vitamin B12 (VB12, >98%), 2,5-diamino­benzenesulfonic acid (Pa-SO3H, >97%), and rifampicin (RFP, >95%) were obtained from Merck (UK). Coumarin 6 (C6, >98%), and bromo­thymol blue (BrB) were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (UK). Methanol, ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, mesitylene, and ethyl acetate were purchased from VWR Chemicals (UK). 1,3,5-Triformylphlorogluci­nol (Tp, >99%), 2,2′-benzidinedisulfonic acid (Bd-(SO3H)2, >70%), noctanoic acid (>98%), 2,4-diphenyl-4-methyl-1-pentene (DMP, >95%), sodium formate (>97%), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, >99%), and eriochrome black T (EBT) were procured from the TCI (Belgium). 5,6-Dimethylben­zimidazole (DBI, >97%) and 1-amino-4-methylpiperazine (AMP, >98%) were purchased from BLDpharm (UK). All chemicals and reagents were used as received without further purification. All the experiments were performed with deionized (DI) water with a conductivity below 18 μS/cm. 2.2. Membrane fabrication In this study, we employ an in-situ IP approach in which the support (or substrate) is fixed at the interface between two immiscible phases, enabling the COF layer to grow directly on one side of the support.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: he support (or substrate) is fixed at the interface between two immiscible phases, enabling the COF layer to grow directly on one side of the support. Because of the presence of the support at the interface, the IP occurs at the pore mouth of the support, which significantly reduces the interfa­cial turbulence until a continuous film is formed through the connection of the small domains. Adding a porous support at the interface can not only stabilize the interface but also decelerate the reaction rate by slowing one of the monomers’ diffusion rates, thereby facilitating the formation of thinner COF layers with higher crystallinity. The TpPa-SO3H membrane was synthesized via the in-situ IP method. During the process, a dual-activation strategy was used, in which sodium formate activated the diamine monomer in the aqueous

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: B. Hao et al. Journal of Membrane Science 746 (2026) 125285 phase by deprotonating the NH3+ groups back to more nucleophilic NH2 groups, whereas n-octanoic acid activated the aldehyde monomers by facilitating the oxygen capture of the –CHO groups, forming –C+HOH groups, thereby increasing its susceptibility to nucleophilic attack [25]. The reactivity of both monomers was significantly enhanced, leading to polymerization at the interface [25]. Here, the synthesis was carried out in a custom-made H-cell, vertically positioned, with a porous nylon support fixed at the interface between the organic and aqueous phases, as shown in Fig. 1, enabling the TpPa-SO3H COF layer to grow directly on the support. The reaction proceeded for 4 days, allowing sufficient time for self-healing and framework reconstruction, thereby minimizing defects and improving the crystallinity of the formed COF film. The TpPa-SO3H membranes with a TpBd-(SO3H)2 interlayer were fabricated via a sequential in situ IP process using the same apparatus. The detailed procedures are described below. COF TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane: As mentioned above, the TpPaSO3H membrane was synthesized in a homemade H-cell through an insitu IP approach. A porous nylon support with a diameter of 41 mm was securely positioned in the middle of the H-cell.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: a homemade H-cell through an insitu IP approach. A porous nylon support with a diameter of 41 mm was securely positioned in the middle of the H-cell. An organic solution containing 42 mg of Tp dissolved in a mixture of 0.1 mL of n-octanoic acid and 20 mL of mesitylene was subsequently added to one side of the H-cell to wet the support first, followed by the addition of 30 mL of an aqueous solution containing 56 mg of Pa-SO3H and 16 mg of sodium formate on the other side. The H-cell was then positioned vertically, ensuring that the organic phase remained on top and the aqueous phase remained at the bottom. The system was maintained at a specific tem­perature (15, 20 or 30 ◦C) in an incubator to facilitate polymerization and membrane growth. Since the nylon support was first wetted by the organic phase, a dark red film was grown on the aqueous phase side after reacting for 4 days as shown in Fig. 1. Finally, the membrane was carefully retrieved by removing both phases and rinsed thoroughly with water and methanol twice each. The resulting membranes were stored in methanol for future use. COF TpPa-SO3H membrane with a TpBd-(SO3H)2 interlayer: The membrane was fabricated through a sequential in-situ IP process. First, a TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer was fabricated on a nylon support using the in-situ IP method.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ane was fabricated through a sequential in-situ IP process. First, a TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer was fabricated on a nylon support using the in-situ IP method. Specifically, 20 mL of an organic phase comprising 63 mg of Tp dissolved in a mixture of 15 mL of n-octanoic acid and 5 mL of ethyl acetate was added to one compartment of the H-cell, while 30 mL of an aqueous solution containing 155 mg of Bd-(SO3H)2 and 32 mg of sodium formate was added to the opposite side. The polymerization was con­ducted at 15 ◦C for 4 days without disturbance. The TpPa-SO3H layer was subsequently grown atop the as-prepared TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer using the aforementioned method at 20 ◦C for 4 days. Finally, the resulting TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon COF membrane was collected after being rinsed with water and methanol twice each and stored in meth­anol for further use. For material characterization purposes, free-standing films or nanosheets of TpPa-SO3H and TpPa-(SO3H)2 were also prepared, and the details are given in the Supplementary Information (SI). 2.3. Characterization Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was carried out using a Cary 630 FT-IR spectrometer to characterize the functional groups in the membranes, with wavenumbers ranging from 4000 to 400 cm-1. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the COF samples were measured at 77 K via a 3Flex instrument. Prior to the measurement, the COF nanosheets were degassed at 120 ◦C overnight under vacuum.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: COF samples were measured at 77 K via a 3Flex instrument. Prior to the measurement, the COF nanosheets were degassed at 120 ◦C overnight under vacuum. The specific surface area was calculated via the Brunauer‒Emmett–Teller (BET) model, and the pore size distributions were obtained based on nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT). The crystallinity of the COF nanosheets was characterized via powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) on an X'Pert PRO Diffractometer (PANalytical). During the measurements, the sample was scanned from 3◦to 40◦with a step size of 0.01◦under a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA. Microscopy images of the COF membranes were captured using a digital microscopy (Keyence VHZ100R). The microstructure of the COF membranes was imaged by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a LEO Gemini 1525 field emission gun SEM (FEGSEM). For cross-sectional imaging, dry mem­branes were fractured after being submerged in liquid nitrogen. The COF membranes were also characterized using a high-resolution trans­mission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with a Titan G2 60-300 electron microscope for high-resolution imaging. 2.4. Membrane performance test The OSN performance of the membranes was tested in a dead-end system with an effective membrane area of 3.14 cm2. A series of per­formance tests were carried out to comprehensively evaluate the COF membranes, including solvent permeation and rejection of various rigid dyes.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: f per­formance tests were carried out to comprehensively evaluate the COF membranes, including solvent permeation and rejection of various rigid dyes. Permeation test: The membranes were pressurized at 5 bar for at least 30 min for stabilization prior to the solvent permeation test. A series of organic solvents were used to test the solvent permeance, which can be calculated as follows: P(L m-2h-1bar-1)=VA * Δt * ΔpEq.1 where V (L) is the volume of permeate through the effective membrane area A (m2) under transmembrane pressure Δp (bar) within time Δt (h). Rejection test: To evaluate the rejection performance of the mem­branes, a mixture of neutral dye molecules was used as a feed solution for separation tests. The mixture contained VB12 (MW: 1355 g/mol), BrB (MW: 624 g/mol), C6 (MW: 350 g/mol), and DMP (MW: 236 g/ mol), with each component at a concentration of 10 mg/L dissolved in methanol. More information of the dye molecules can be found in Table S1. The dye mixture was measured via an Agilent high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) system equipped with a diode array detector (DAD, 1260 Infinity II). Separation was performed on a reversed-phase Hichrom Ace column (Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18, 3.0 * 150 mm, 2.7 μm), with gradient elution of two mobile phases: 0.1 wt% ammonium acetate in water (phase A) and methanol (phase B).

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ell 120 EC-C18, 3.0 * 150 mm, 2.7 μm), with gradient elution of two mobile phases: 0.1 wt% ammonium acetate in water (phase A) and methanol (phase B). Specifically, the gradient began with 90% A held isocratically for 2 min, followed by a linear decrease to 5% A over 15 min, maintained for 4 min, and then restored to 90% A at 21.01 min, with 4 min of re-equilibration before the next run. The characteristic peaks of VB12, BrB, C6, and DMP can be observed at retention times of 9.2 min (λ = 241 nm), 17.4 min (λ = 425 nm), 19.0 min (λ = 451 nm), and 20.1 min (λ = 241 nm), respectively. 2.5. Pharmaceutical fractionation To evaluate the fractionation performance of the membranes for pharmaceutical compounds and their precursors, three pairs of sub­stances (VB12/DBI, RFP/AMP, and EBT/4-NP) were used as feed solu­tions. More information of the pharmaceutical and precursor molecules can be found in Table S2. Each pair consisted of a pharmaceutical and its corresponding pre­cursor, both of which were dissolved in methanol at a concentration of 20 mg/L. After filtration through the membranes, the feed, permeate, and retentate solutions were analyzed via UV‒Vis spectroscopy (NanoDrop UV‒Visible Spectrophotometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The final rejection of each solute was calculated using the following equation: Rejection (%) =(1 -Cp Cf )* 100%Eq.2 where Cp and Cf are the concentrations of the permeate and feed solu­tions, respectively.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content:
B. Hao et al. Journal of Membrane Science 746 (2026) 125285

3. Results and discussion 3.1. TpPa-SO3H/nylon membranes Characterization: Microscopic imaging of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane synthesized via in-situ IP revealed a uniform, continuous dark-red surface without visible defects at the microscale, as shown in Fig. 2A. The successful formation of the ketoenamine-linked TpPa-SO3H COF illustrated in Fig. 2B was confirmed by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 2C, the emergence of characteristic C––C (1570 cm-1) and C–N

(1230 cm-1) stretching vibrations in the TpPa-SO3H spectrum, together with the disappearance of the N–H (3300–3400 cm-1) and C––O (1638 cm-1) bands present in the monomers, verifies the formation of the desired network [26]. The crystallinity of the COF layer in the TpPa-SO3H/nylon mem­brane is difficult to check via X-ray diffraction (XRD) because of its extremely thin layer and existence of the support, which has a strong background signal that might hinder the diffraction peaks of COFs. Therefore, a self-standing COF film fabricated under the same conditions was used instead to evaluate the intrinsic crystallinity and stacking

Fig. 2. (A) Microscopy image of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane; (B) Schematic illustration showing the formation of the TpPa-SO3H COF; (C) FT-IR spectra of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane and corresponding monomers.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: Fig. 3. (A) Experimental XRD patterns compared with simulated XRD patterns of the TpPa-SO3H self-standing film; (B) HR-TEM image of the TpPa-SO3H selfstanding film with its FFT pattern and (C) Lattice intensity profile. B. Hao et al. Journal of Membrane Science 746 (2026) 125285 structure. As shown in Fig. 3A, the experimental XRD pattern exhibited a strong diffraction peak at 4.8◦, corresponding to the (100) plane, indi­cating the presence of long-range in-plane order [27]. Additionally, a broad peak observed between 24◦and 28◦is attributed to π–π stacking interactions between the benzene rings of adjacent layers [28]. These results are in good agreement with the simulated XRD pattern based on the inclined AA-stacking model shown in Fig. S1, as confirmed by Pawley refinement. HR-TEM was also used to visualize the structural regularity of the as-synthesized TpPa-SO3H self-standing film. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 3B) together with its corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (inset of Fig. 3B) reveal well-ordered lattice fringes corresponding to the (002) plane [29], confirming the high crystallinity of the TpPa-SO3H framework. The lattice intensity profile given in Fig. 3C indicates a regular interlayer spacing of approximately 0.30 nm. The results of further characterization of the TpPa-SO3H selfstanding films by N2 adsorption‒desorption isotherms (Fig. S2) and SEM (Fig. S3) are given in the SI.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: of further characterization of the TpPa-SO3H selfstanding films by N2 adsorption‒desorption isotherms (Fig. S2) and SEM (Fig. S3) are given in the SI. All the above characterization results suggested the successful synthesis of TpPa-SO3H membranes with welldefined framework structures and high crystallinity. Synthesis at different temperatures: The polymerization reaction temperatures significantly affect the formation of TpPa-SO3H COF membranes during the in-situ IP process. Three representative room temperatures (15, 20, and 30 ◦C) were chosen for comparison. Since the organic phase was used to wet the nylon support first, the COF layer formed on the aqueous-phase side through the in-situ IP process. Fig. 4A–C shows the porous structures of the COF membrane surfaces prepared at different reaction temperatures. Beneath the porous surface, there was a skin layer, as shown in Fig. 4D–F, which likely performs molecular sieving through the pores of the COFs. This asymmetric structure was further confirmed by the SEM image of the free-standing film synthesized under the same conditions (Fig. S3–a), which revealed a loose morphology on the aqueous side and a dense and smooth structure on the side facing the organic phase. This phenomenon is consistent with observations reported by previous investigators [28,30].

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: and smooth structure on the side facing the organic phase. This phenomenon is consistent with observations reported by previous investigators [28,30]. This asymmetry arises because the amine monomers diffuse much more rapidly from the aqueous phase, leading to a higher local reaction rate and rough, defect-rich growth on the water-facing side. In contrast, the slower diffusion of aldehyde monomers from the organic phase re­sults in a more controlled reaction and the formation of a dense and smoother layer on the organic-facing side [30]. The skin layer thicknesses estimated from Fig. 4D–F increased from approximately 100 nm to 230 nm and 390 nm as the polymerization temperature increased from 15 ◦C to 20 ◦C and 30 ◦C, respectively. At elevated temperatures, the surface tension and viscosity of mesitylene decrease, which enhances the solubility of the diamine monomer and

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: B. Hao et al. Journal of Membrane Science 746 (2026) 125285 promotes its diffusion from the aqueous phase into the organic phase [31]. In addition, higher temperatures can swell the initially formed COF film, weakening its self-limiting growth behaviour [31]. Collec­tively, these effects increase the availability of diamine monomers within the reaction zone, accelerate polymerization, and ultimately lead to the formation of thicker COF skin layers at higher reaction temper­atures. It should be noted that the skin layer thickness of an asymmetric membrane is inherently difficult to measure accurately [32]. Therefore, the thicknesses estimated in Fig. 4D–F should only be regarded as indicative values, provided solely for ease of comparison. A further comparison of COF membranes prepared with and without a support reveals that the skin layer thickness decreased markedly from 780 nm for the self-standing film (Fig. S3–b) to 230 nm (Fig. 4E) when a nylon support was introduced into the IP process under otherwise identical reaction conditions. This result indicates that the support can regulate monomer diffusion, thereby yielding a substantially thinner skin layer. This phenomenon further confirms the critical role of the support and the effectiveness of the in-situ IP method for COF membrane fabrication, which motivated us to develop a sequential in-situ IP strategy for constructing the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon mem­brane, which will be discussed in section 3.2.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ed us to develop a sequential in-situ IP strategy for constructing the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon mem­brane, which will be discussed in section 3.2. Performance test: Separation of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane synthesized at different temperatures was evaluated by dye rejection as shown in Fig. 5A. All three membranes exhibited high rejections above 90% for dye molecules with a molecular weight >300 g mol-1 and low rejection for DMP with a molecular weight of 236 g mol-1. No signifi­cant differences were observed among the three membranes for the rejection of VB12, BrB, or C6. However, for DMP, which is the smallest dye molecule among the four dyes, the rejection increased from 34.0% ± 2.8% (15 ◦C) to 49.0% ± 2.8% (20 ◦C) and further to 55.5% ± 14.9% (30 ◦C). These results indicate that membrane thickness plays a crucial role in separation performance. The improvement in rejection with increasing thickness of the COF layer can be attributed to the narrowed pore size triggered by the arbitrary layer shifting in thicker membranes [33]. With respect to solvent permeation, a marked reduction in solvent permeance was observed as the membrane thickness increased with increasing reaction temperatures as shown in Fig. 5B. For example, in the case of methanol, the permeance decreased from 76.3 ± 3.7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 for the 100 nm (15 ◦C) membrane to 21.7 ± 2.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 at a thickness of 230 nm (20 ◦C) and further to 5.1 ± 0.6 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 at a thickness of 390 nm (30 ◦C).

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: ◦C) membrane to 21.7 ± 2.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 at a thickness of 230 nm (20 ◦C) and further to 5.1 ± 0.6 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 at a thickness of 390 nm (30 ◦C). A similar trend was observed for ethanol, where the permeance decreased from 50.0 ± 3.7 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 to 18.8 ± 1.3 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and 2.0 ± 0.1 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 with increasing thickness. Membrane stability is one of the key criteria for evaluating the per­formance of a membrane over the long term. The TpPa-SO3H/nylon membranes synthesized at 15 ◦C and 20 ◦C were subjected to a 36-h rejection test using VB12. The membrane prepared at 20 ◦C exhibited good stability, maintaining rejection above 98% throughout the test, whereas the 15 ◦C membrane became unstable, showing a decrease in rejection from 98.93% to 88.78% after 24 h, as shown in Fig. S4. This decline can be attributed to the ultrathin selective layer, which was more susceptible to disruption during prolonged testing.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: 3.2. TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane Characterization: To improve the permeance of the COF membrane without compromising rejection, a TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane was fabricated by first synthesizing an interlayer layer of TpBd-(SO3H)2 on a nylon support, followed by a layer of TpPa-SO3H fabricated at 20 ◦C via sequential in-situ IP as illustrated in Fig. 6A. TpBd-(SO3H)2 is also a sulfonated COF (Fig. S5–a). The microscopy images reveal that the TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer had a light-yellow surface (Fig. 6B), whereas the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane appeared orange (Fig. 6C), reflecting the combination of the two COF layers. It is worth to mention that the same aldehyde monomer was used in the synthesis of both COF layers, which is crucial for ensuring structural similarity and enhancing the compatibility required to form a coherent layered architecture. The structural and chemical properties of TpBd-(SO3H)2 were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. S5–b), XRD (Fig. S5–c) and BET analysis (Fig. S5-d, e), indicating the formation of β-ketoen­amine linkages, high crystallinity, a surface area of 99.77 m2/g and a calculated pore size of 2.5 nm, which is larger than that of TpPa-SO3H (1.5 nm, shown in Fig. S2–b). Unlike the porous morphology of the TpPa-SO3H membrane, the

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

Fig. 6. TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer demonstrated a relatively compact surface (Fig. 6D) with a thickness of approximately 120 nm (Fig. 6E). The performance of this interlayer (TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon) was evaluated through PEG rejection and solvent permeation tests, details of which were given in SI. As can be seen in Fig. S6–a, the interlayer alone resulted in low PEG rejection, with rejections below 20% for all the PEG molecules, while exhibiting high solvent permeance, for example, 526 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 Fig. 7. citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: B. Hao et al. Journal of Membrane Science 746 (2026) 125285 for acetonitrile and 304 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 for methanol (Fig. S6–b). Following its formation, a TpPa-SO3H layer was subsequently syn­thesized on top via the same in-situ IP method. Owing to the smaller pore size of TpBd-(SO3H)2 than that of the nylon support, the monomer diffusion rate is expected to be restricted during the second growth step. As a result, the upper TpPa-SO3H layer exhibited a compact morphology (Fig. 6F) instead of a loose and porous structure. The actual thickness of the TpPa-SO3H layer cannot be directly determined because it is fully integrated with the TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer, resulting in a combined thickness of 254 nm, as shown in Fig. 6G. Consequently, the thickness of the TpPa-SO3H layer is estimated to be 134 nm by subtracting the thickness of the TpBd-(SO3H)2 layer from the total thickness measured above the nylon support (Fig. 6G). Compared with the TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon interlayer, the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd- (SO3H)2/nylon membrane exhibits an enhanced PEG rejection, with an estimated MWCO of approximately 5000 g mol-1, indicating improved size-sieving performance (Fig. S7). XRD patterns of the TpPa-SO3H/ TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane indicates the coexistence of both highly crystalline COFs, as evidenced by the (100) distinct diffraction peaks at 3.8◦and 4.7◦, corresponding to TpPa-SO3H and TpBd-(SO3H)2, respec­tively as shown in Fig. S8.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: as evidenced by the (100) distinct diffraction peaks at 3.8◦and 4.7◦, corresponding to TpPa-SO3H and TpBd-(SO3H)2, respec­tively as shown in Fig. S8. Performance test: To evaluate the performance of the TpPa-SO3H/ TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane, dye rejection and solvent permeation tests were conducted. These results were compared with the perfor­mance of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane prepared under the same conditions at 20 ◦C. Compared with the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane, the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane exhibited comparable dye rejection, as evidenced by their nearly overlapping separation curves as shown in Fig. 7A. In terms of solvent permeances given in Fig. 7B, the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane presented higher values in several solvents (acetonitrile: 24.9 ± 5.4 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, methanol: 42.8 L ± 4.7 m-2 h-1 bar-1, acetone: 14.1 ± 3.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, ethanol: 22.8 ± 6.0 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) than did the TpPaSO3H/nylon (20 ◦C) membrane (acetonitrile: 17.6 ± 2.6 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, methanol: 21.7 ± 2.8 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, acetone: 5.4 ± 0.2 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, ethanol: 18.8 ± 1.3 L m-2 h-1 bar-1). The enhanced solvent permeance can be attributed to the combined effects of the thinner TpPa-SO3H selective layer and the underlying TpBd-(SO3H)2 interlayer, which acts as a gutter layer by providing additional lateral transport pathways, together leading to the increased solvent permeance.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: 3H)2 interlayer, which acts as a gutter layer by providing additional lateral transport pathways, together leading to the increased solvent permeance. Compared with commercial membranes, reported COF membranes and other start-of-the-art membranes, the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane and the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane prepared at 20 ◦C in this work showed superior performance, surpassing the upper bound, in terms of methanol permeance and dye rejection of the molecules between 327 and 408 g/mol, as shown in Fig. 7C and Table S3. 3.3. Pharmaceutical fractionation Three pharmaceutical–precursor pairs were selected: VB12/DBI, RFP/AMP, and EBT/4-NP and their chemical structures are given in Table S2. It can be seen from Fig. 8A and Figs. S9 that the TpPa-SO3H/ nylon membrane exhibited high rejection of all three pharmaceuticals—95.8% ± 0.8% for VB12, 99.0% ± 1.4% for RFP, and 95.4% ± 1.2% for EBT, while exhibiting relatively low rejection of their corresponding precursors, with values of 9.4 ± 6.3%, 8.8% ± 0.9%, and 15.3% ± 0.1%, respectively. In this process, the pharmaceuticals (VB12, RFP, and EBT), which have relatively high molecular weights, were retained by the COF separation layer, while their relatively small pre­cursors (DBI, AMP, and 4-NP) tended to pass through the membrane, thereby enabling effective discrimination of the two components.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: elatively small pre­cursors (DBI, AMP, and 4-NP) tended to pass through the membrane, thereby enabling effective discrimination of the two components. These results demonstrate the superior selectivity of the TpPa-SO3H layer in achieving precise molecular separation in pharmaceutical fractionation. The same improvement was also observed in the pharmaceuticalprecursor fractionation for the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon mem­brane. For example, in the example of VB12 and its precursor DBI, the

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane and the TpPa-SO3H/nylon (20 ◦C) membrane achieved comparably high rejection of VB12 (96.5% ± 0.2% vs. 95.8% ± 0.8%) and low rejection of DBI (11.6% ± 2.2% vs. 9.4% ± 6.3%) (Fig. 8A, Fig. S9). Despite of a slightly higher DBI rejec­tion by the TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane, it delivered a much higher permeance for the pharmaceutical mixture (8.4 ± 0.7 vs. 3.0 ± 0.6 L m-2 h-1 bar-1) as shown in Fig. 8B. Similar trends were also observed for the other two pharmaceutical mixtures where the per­meance was significantly improved by approximately 2 times while maintaining comparable selectivity (Fig. 8B). A stability test further confirmed the robustness of the TpPa-SO3H/ TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane, with VB12 rejection remaining high (>97%) and DBI rejection remaining low (<10%) throughout 4 days as shown in Fig. 8C. Post-test SEM characterization (Fig. S10) reveals that the membrane maintained an intact and continuous morphology, further confirming its structural integrity and operational durability. 4. Conclusions In this work, TpPa-SO3H/nylon and TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/ nylon membranes were successfully fabricated via a reliable in-situ IP strategy, enabling the direct formation of thin and highly crystalline COF membranes on porous supports.

citation.title: High-performance COF membranes prepared by an in-situ interfacial polymerization for effective pharmaceutical fractionation

content: essfully fabricated via a reliable in-situ IP strategy, enabling the direct formation of thin and highly crystalline COF membranes on porous supports. The separation performance of the TpPa-SO3H/nylon membrane can be effectively tuned by polymeriza­tion temperature, achieving high rejection of pharmaceuticals (VB12, RFP, and EBT) while allowing smaller precursors (DBI, AMP, and 4-NP) to permeate, thereby enabling precise molecular fractionation. To mitigate the permeability–selectivity trade-off, a hierarchical TpPa-SO3H/TpBd-(SO3H)2/nylon membrane was constructed by intro­ducing a mesoporous COF interlayer beneath a microporous selective layer. This architecture significantly enhanced solvent permeance, nearly doubling the methanol permeance compared with the singlelayer membrane, while maintaining comparable rejection and longterm operational stability. The use of two structurally compatible sul­fonated COFs with different pore sizes provides a versatile platform for tailoring membrane performance. Overall, this study demonstrates an effective strategy for designing high-performance COF membranes with a favorable balance between permeance and selectivity, highlighting their potential for efficient OSN and high-value pharmaceutical separations. Zhiwei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.
